ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for grilled lamb midloin chop; SNOMED CT coverage for specific cooked meat cuts is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one grilled lamb midloin chop with bone, approximately 120 g, yielding roughly 80–90 g of edible lean and fat meat after accounting for bone weight.

nutrient_source.title: Grilled lamb midloin chop — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for grilled lamb midloin chop (food code F06050002). All core macros and micronutrients reflect the HPB-reported values. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for lamb loin chop, cooked, broiled (NDB 17066) as HPB does not separately report these for this item; this is documented as an adapted value.

gi_source.title: N/A — Food contains negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index is not applicable for this food as it contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. No glycaemic response is expected. GL per serving is 0.0 by definition. This is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database's approach for pure protein/fat foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (64 mg/100 g) is favourable; however, saturated fat (~7.4 g/100 g) exceeds the AHA 5 g/100 g threshold and may contribute to arterial stiffness with regular high intake.
hypertension.guidance: Grilled lamb midloin chop is acceptable for hypertensive individuals in moderate portions (≤120 g per meal) provided no salt or high-sodium sauces are added; trimming visible fat before grilling further reduces saturated fat load and supports vascular health.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrates and zero sugar produce no glycaemic impact; high protein (26.8 g/100 g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability, though saturated fat at ~7.4 g/100 g may worsen insulin resistance if consumed in excess.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled lamb midloin chop is suitable for type 2 diabetes management as a protein source with no glycaemic load; limit portion to 100–120 g, trim visible fat, avoid sugary marinades, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to maintain a balanced meal per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~7.4 g/100 g) and ruminant trans fat (~0.4 g/100 g) are both atherogenic; cholesterol at 88 mg/100 g adds to LDL burden; however, the food is low in sodium and provides iron and potassium.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit grilled lamb midloin chop to occasional consumption (≤1–2 times per week), choose lean cuts with visible fat trimmed, avoid added salt or butter-based sauces, and balance with omega-3-rich foods such as oily fish to offset the pro-atherogenic saturated and trans fat content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High arachidonic acid content from red meat drives RA inflammatory pathways; saturated fat (~7.4 g/100 g) exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; ruminant trans fat (~0.4 g/100 g) is strongly pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Grilled lamb midloin chop is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its high saturated fat, ruminant trans fat, and arachidonic acid content; substitute with oily fish (e.g., salmon, sardines) to obtain protein with anti-inflammatory omega-3 benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled lamb midloin chop is a bone-in cut of lamb taken from the loin section, positioned between the rib and leg. The midloin chop contains a T-shaped bone with lean muscle meat on both sides and a rim of external fat, plus internal marbling. When grilled, the exterior develops a slightly charred, caramelised crust from the Maillard reaction while the interior remains juicy and tender. The flavour is distinctly rich, slightly gamey, and savoury — characteristic of lamb compared to beef or pork. Per 100 g, it delivers 276 kcal, 26.8 g protein, 18.9 g fat (of which approximately 7.4 g is saturated and 0.4 g is ruminant trans fat), 0 g carbohydrate, 64 mg sodium, 281 mg potassium, 10 mg calcium, 2.33 mg iron, and 88 mg cholesterol. The zero carbohydrate content makes it suitable for ketogenic and low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. It is halal-certified when sourced from approved abattoirs and is a common protein option in Singaporean mamak restaurants and hawker settings. Clinically, the high saturated fat and ruminant trans fat content are concerns for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis management, while the low sodium and zero carbohydrate profile are favourable for hypertension and type 2 diabetes respectively. Iron content (2.33 mg/100 g) supports haemoglobin synthesis and is relevant for individuals at risk of iron-deficiency anaemia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a grilled lamb midloin chop?
Is lamb chop suitable for diabetes?
Is lamb chop high in saturated fat?
Can I eat lamb chop if I have high blood pressure?
Lamb midloin chop vs lamb rack nutrition

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (food code F06050002) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 17066, lamb loin chop cooked broiled) as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this item; this adaptation is documented. All other values are exact HPB matches.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as lamb midloin chop is a pure animal protein and fat food with no plant-derived carbohydrate content. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as dietary fibre is absent in all animal muscle tissue. Confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo (F06050002) and consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 17066). Saturated_fat_g (7.4 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (7.8 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.2 g), and trans_fat_g (0.4 g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17066 (lamb loin chop, cooked, broiled) as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this food code. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed data for these nutrients is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for food code F06050002 as of 2026-04-16, and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17066 was not used for these fields to avoid mixing source granularity across nutrient categories: vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Source consulted and confirmed unavailable for this specific food code: HPB Singapore Foodo database (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/, accessed 2026-04-16). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g; glycemic_index set to null and gi_category set to not_applicable per Veritas protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods. FoodEx2 code A0CKM used as the closest defensible match for lamb meat loin grilled; SNOMED CT code not assigned due to limited coverage of specific cooked meat cuts in SNOMED CT as of 2026.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal 276.0, protein_g 26.8, fat_g 18.9, carbohydrate_g 0.0 all present and numeric. Source is HPB Singapore Foodo (F06050002). Zero carbohydrate justified as naturally zero in notes.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 0.0 (naturally zero, justified), sodium_mg 64.0, saturated_fat_g 7.4 (adapted from USDA SR Legacy NDB 17066, documented), sugar_g 0.0 (naturally zero, justified). All present as numeric values.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to null (not 0) per Veritas protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods, gi_category set to not_applicable, glycemic_load_per_serving 0.0, gi_source documents rationale. Food contains 0 g carbohydrate; GI not applicable.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI and label present for lamb chop food product. FoodEx2 code A0CKM assigned for lamb meat loin grilled. SNOMED CT null with documented gap note. No invented codes.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, covers food identity, preparation, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate), Type 2 Diabetes (low), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance. human_condition_name for type2_diabetes is 'Type 2 Diabetes'. Additional condition rheumatoid_arthritis included.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases flat array contains all strings from aliases_by_language. English variants, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and romanized forms all present. Consistency self-check passed: all values in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft produced from input JSON.
Iteration 2: Corrected gi_source.source_match_type to 'analogous_estimate', documented -1 fields in notes, added Kao yang yao pai to flat aliases array.

Iteration 3: Expanded notes to explicitly document every -1 field in per_100g with the specific reason for data unavailability and the source consulted that confirmed unavailability (HPB Singapore Foodo, accessed 2026-04-16), resolving G3 hard gate failure. Confirmed glycemic_index remains null (not a numeric value) to satisfy Q4 requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric only when a GI value exists — null is the correct sentinel for not-applicable foods per Veritas protocol.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Record passes all hard gates and scores 8/8. G3 failure from iteration 2 resolved by explicitly documenting all 15 fields set to -1 in the notes section, naming the source consulted (HPB Singapore Foodo, food code F06050002) and confirming data unavailability as of 2026-04-16. glycemic_index correctly set to null for a zero-carbohydrate food per Veritas protocol. Core macros sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo with high confidence. Fatty acid fractions adapted from USDA SR Legacy NDB 17066 and documented. Zero carbohydrate, sugar, and fibre justified as naturally zero. Mandatory Singaporean multilingual names present. All three required health conditions included with clinical guidance. Ontology anchors defensible with no invented codes.
